data.title: AutoNation (AN) Heads Into Q1 2026 With Net Margin Squeezed To 2.3% Narrative Test
data.description:
AutoNation (AN) opened Q1 2026 earnings season with context shaped by recent quarterly numbers, including Q4 2025 revenue of US$6.9b, basic EPS of US$4.70, and net income of US$172.1m, alongside trailing 12 month EPS of US$17.26 on US$27.6b of revenue and US$649.1m of net income. Over the past year, the company has seen quarterly revenue move between US$6.7b and US$7.0b, while basic EPS ranged from US$2.29 to US$5.72. Trailing 12 month revenue edged from US$27.0b to US$27.6b as EPS held in the mid teens, setting up a results story where modest earnings growth expectations sit against margins that have compressed and now sit under closer scrutiny.

See our full analysis for AutoNation.

With the numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the key market narratives around AutoNation's growth potential, risk profile, and margin trajectory.

See what the community is saying about AutoNation

Advertisement

Margins Tighten as Net Profit Slips to 2.3%

  • Over the last 12 months, AutoNation converted US$27.6b of revenue into US$649.1m of net income, which works out to a 2.3% net margin compared with 2.6% a year earlier.
  • Supporters of the bullish view point to expanding After Sales and finance operations as potential margin support, yet the reported margin trend highlights a few pressure points:
    • After Sales is described as high margin and growing, but the step down from a 2.6% to a 2.3% net margin shows that, in aggregate, costs or mix shifts have leaned against that positive story over the last year.
    • Bullish arguments around AN Finance and digital efficiency rely on higher profitability over time, while the current margin data show that, so far, the overall earnings line has not yet reflected that improvement.

Bulls argue these compressed margins could be a starting point for improvement rather than a ceiling, which is why many still focus on the longer term upside potential in service and finance income. 🐂 AutoNation Bull Case

Debt Coverage Risk Sits Behind Q4 Profit Figures

  • AutoNation earned US$172.1m of net income in Q4 2025 and US$649.1m over the last 12 months, while risk analysis highlights that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow.
  • Investors aligned with the bearish narrative will likely focus on how this balance sheet flag interacts with earnings trends:
    • Bears already point to pressures from higher tooling and training costs for hybrids and EVs, and the margin move from 2.6% to 2.3% suggests that extra operating demands have not been fully absorbed by revenue growth.
    • Concerns about holding elevated used vehicle inventory also tie back to this debt coverage risk, because slower turns or weaker used pricing could make it harder for earnings and cash flow to comfortably support leverage.

Skeptics see this weaker cash flow coverage as a key reason to stress test how much downside protection the current earnings base really offers. 🐻 AutoNation Bear Case

P/E of 11.1x Versus 20.3x Industry Multiple

  • At a P/E of 11.1x on trailing earnings of US$17.26 per share, AutoNation trades below the US Specialty Retail industry multiple of 20.3x, peer average of 16x, and a reported broader US market P/E of 19.4x.
  • The consensus style narrative that AutoNation combines steady After Sales growth with rising digital and finance contributions meets a mixed valuation signal:
    • On one hand, forecast earnings growth of about 3.8% per year and a lower than industry P/E suggest the current US$210 share price is not baking in aggressive expectations compared with the analyst target of US$236.64.
    • On the other hand, the same data set flags a five year earnings decline of 14.5% per year and thinner 2.3% margins, which helps explain why the market may be assigning a discount despite those growth and After Sales narratives.

data.title: DexCom (DXCM) Margin Improvement Challenges Cautious Narratives After Q1 2026 Earnings
data.description:
DexCom (DXCM) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of US$1.2b and basic EPS of US$0.52, set against trailing twelve month revenue of US$4.8b and EPS of US$2.39 that came with a net profit margin of 19.3% compared with 12.9% a year earlier. Over the past year, revenue has moved from US$4.0b to US$4.8b and EPS from US$1.46 to US$2.39, alongside earnings growth of 73.8% that analysts in the supplied data expect to continue. This puts the spotlight firmly on how durable these margins look after the latest print.

See our full analysis for DexCom.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to weigh them against the most common narratives around DexCom to see which stories the results support and which ones start to look out of date.

See what the community is saying about DexCom

Advertisement

Margins and Profit Share Tell the Story

  • Over the last twelve months, DexCom converted US$4.8b of revenue into US$930.4m of net income, which works out to a 19.3% net margin compared with 12.9% a year earlier in the supplied data.
  • Bears focus on whether margins can keep rising. Yet their own case still assumes net income moves from US$930.4m to about US$1.4b by 2029,
    • They also assume net margin eventually reaches 21.8%, which is higher than the current 19.3% and would represent further profitability expansion if it plays out as described.
    • At the same time, they assume the P/E settles at 21.6x compared with the current 25.4x. Their caution is therefore more about what investors are willing to pay for those earnings than about the earnings level itself.
DexCom's recent margin profile sets a clear benchmark for those cautious assumptions, and the bearish case leans heavily on valuation multiples rather than an earnings collapse, which is important context if you are testing how conservative that view really is.🐻 DexCom Bear Case

Revenue Growth vs Coverage Opportunity

  • On the forecast numbers provided, revenue is expected to grow around 10.6% per year compared with about 11% for the broader US market, while bearish analysts in the narrative assume 9.8% annual revenue growth to reach roughly US$6.4b by 2029.
  • Critics highlight that even though commercial coverage already extends to more than 6 million type 2 non insulin patients and is expected to exceed 7 million with Prime Therapeutics,
    • They point out that roughly two thirds of those covered patients are not yet using continuous glucose monitoring, so the adoption gap could hold revenue growth closer to the high single digits cited in their 9.8% assumption.
    • They also flag that timing around possible CMS coverage and tender outcomes outside the US could influence how quickly that larger eligible population shows up in the reported revenue figures.

High Growth Meets Premium Valuation

  • Trailing earnings grew 73.8% over the past year with EPS on a trailing basis at US$2.39, and the stock trades on a P/E of about 25.4x at a share price of US$61.35, while the provided DCF fair value is US$73.21 and the allowed analyst price target reference is US$83.92.
  • Consensus narrative points to strong drivers behind that premium multiple, yet the current data gives you a way to check how much is already reflected in the price,
    • On one side, analysts in the supplied figures expect earnings to grow about 16.8% per year, which is consistent with a company that already has a 19.3% net margin and trailing revenue of US$4.8b.
    • On the other, the P/E being above both peer and industry averages, at 25.4x compared with 21.3x and 23.8x respectively, means any slowdown relative to the 10.6% revenue growth forecast could matter more for future valuation than for the business itself.
If you want to see how different investors link this earnings profile to long term expectations, it is worth reading both the optimistic and cautious takes side by side.🐂 DexCom Bull Case

data.title: Cable One (CABO) Q1 Profit Revives Margin Recovery Narrative Despite Heavy Trailing Losses
data.description:
Cable One (CABO) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of US$353 million and basic EPS of US$6.29, alongside net income of US$35.8 million, setting a cleaner starting point after a volatile run of quarterly results. Over the past year, the company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$380.6 million in Q1 2025 and US$387.2 million in Q4 2024 to US$353 million most recently, while trailing twelve month EPS shifted from US$2.58 to a loss of US$57.19. With the share price at US$75.01 and profitability still tight on a trailing basis, this update keeps margins and the path back to more stable earnings firmly in focus for investors assessing the latest release.

See our full analysis for Cable One.

With the headline figures presented, the next step is to compare these results with the key narratives around Cable One to see which stories the numbers support and which they call into question.

See what the community is saying about Cable One

Advertisement

Losses Still Heavy On A 12‑Month View

  • Over the last twelve months, Cable One reported total revenue of US$1,473.8 million and a net loss of US$323.3 million, which works out to trailing EPS of US$57.19 in the red.
  • Bears argue that five years of losses growing at about 59% a year show a business under real earnings pressure, and the latest trailing figures line up with that concern:
    • Net income moved from a loss of US$20.3 million on US$1,555.8 million of revenue a year ago to a loss of US$323.3 million on US$1,473.8 million of revenue most recently, so profitability has been weak even as revenue stayed in the US$1.4 billion to US$1.6 billion range.
    • With Q1 2026 alone showing net income of US$35.8 million on US$353.0 million of revenue, the short term improvement still sits against a longer stretch of losses that bearish investors see as central to their thesis.

Bears often point to this mix of recent profit and heavy trailing losses as a warning sign, and the detailed cautious case sets out how that could play out further. 🐻 Cable One Bear Case

Q1 Profit Contrast With Prior Swings

  • Q1 2026 basic EPS of US$6.29 and net income of US$35.8 million contrast with losses in Q4 2025, when EPS was US$1.35 in the red on a net loss of US$7.6 million, and with a much larger loss in Q2 2025 when EPS was US$77.70 in the red on a net loss of US$438.0 million.
  • Bullish investors argue that these quarterly numbers support a repair story for margins over time, and the data partly backs that but with clear caveats:
    • The bullish narrative expects profit margins to shift from roughly 23.7% in the red to 8.5% in the black within three years. Q1’s profit is a step in that direction, though the trailing net margin is still deeply negative given the US$323.3 million loss on US$1,473.8 million of revenue.
    • Forecasts in the dataset call for earnings growth of about 48.03% a year, yet revenue is expected to slip about 0.8% a year, so the path the bulls highlight leans heavily on better profitability rather than a bigger top line.

The bullish story leans hard on this kind of margin recovery, and the full optimistic case walks through how that could translate into future earnings. 🐂 Cable One Bull Case

Low 0.3x P/S Versus DCF Fair Value

  • At a share price of US$75.01 and a P/S of 0.3x compared with 1.1x for the US Media industry and 2.8x for peers, Cable One screens as inexpensive next to both its sector and an internal DCF fair value of US$275.60.
  • Supporters of the bullish side see this gap as a core part of the opportunity, but the data also reminds you why the discount exists:
    • The DCF fair value of US$275.60 is much higher than US$75.01, yet the company is currently loss making on a trailing basis with five year losses increasing at about 59% annually, so the modelled upside depends on that forecast earnings recovery arriving as expected.
    • At the same time, a major flagged risk is that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow, which sits alongside recent share price volatility and keeps the low P/S and large DCF gap tied to real balance sheet and cash coverage questions rather than simply being a market oversight.

data.title: WisdomTree (WT) Net Margin Jump To 22% Tests Bullish Growth Narrative
data.description:
WisdomTree (WT) opened 2026 with Q1 results anchored by Q4 2025 revenue of US$147.4 million and basic EPS of US$0.29, rounding out a trailing twelve month profile of US$493.8 million in revenue and EPS of US$0.77. Over the past year, revenue has moved from US$427.7 million to US$493.8 million while trailing EPS has gone from US$0.34 to US$0.77, setting the backdrop for how you read the latest print. With trailing net income of US$108.4 million and a net margin profile described at 22% versus 11.7% a year earlier, the story now hinges on how durable those margins look as growth expectations cool from last year’s pace.

See our full analysis for WisdomTree.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to compare these results with the widely held narratives around WisdomTree’s growth, profitability, and risk profile to see which stories still hold up and which look out of date.

See what the community is saying about WisdomTree

Advertisement

Triple digit earnings growth and 22% margin put recent momentum in focus

  • Over the last 12 months, WisdomTree generated trailing net income of US$108.4 million on revenue of US$493.8 million, which works out to a 22% net margin compared with 11.7% a year earlier and earnings growth of 117.4% over the same period.
  • Consensus narrative points to growth driven by expansion into private assets, international markets, digital finance, and alternative products, and these trailing results give that view some support but also raise questions:
    • On one hand, record trailing revenue of US$493.8 million alongside higher margins lines up with the idea that broader product lines and record AUM are feeding through to stronger profitability.
    • On the other hand, forward earnings growth forecasts around 11.15% per year and revenue growth of about 7.8% per year sit well below the very strong trailing earnings growth rate. Anyone leaning on the bullish narrative needs to be comfortable that the recent jump is not a one off.

AUM inflows and fee pressure sit behind quarterly swings

  • Through 2025, quarterly data show AUM moving from US$109.8 billion at the start of Q1 to US$144.5 billion by the end of Q4, with net inflows of US$3,048 million, US$3,527 million, and US$2,237 million in Q1, Q2, and Q3, against quarterly net income in a range of US$18.98 million to US$40.02 million.
  • Bulls argue that early digital infrastructure, regulatory leadership, and unique assets like Ceres Partners farmland position WisdomTree for outsized revenue growth and resilient earnings, and the AUM and earnings profile partly backs that up but also shows some friction:
    • Growing AUM and positive net inflows across 2025 are consistent with bulls’ focus on adviser adoption and broader product demand, especially when paired with trailing TTM revenue moving from US$427.7 million to US$493.8 million.
    • At the same time, quarterly EPS bouncing between US$0.13 and US$0.29 alongside industry wide fee compression reminds you that higher AUM does not automatically translate into steadily rising per share earnings, which is an important check on the more optimistic scenario.
On top of that, if you want to see how bullish analysts connect these inflows, digital assets, and farmland exposure into a longer term story for WT, 🐂 WisdomTree Bull Case

Mixed valuation and debt coverage keep the bears in the conversation

  • WisdomTree currently trades on a trailing P/E of 21x, which is below the US Capital Markets industry average of 42.4x but slightly above the peer average of 19x, while debt is described as not well covered by operating cash flow, pointing to a key balance sheet risk alongside the current share price of US$16.62 and an analyst price target reference of US$19.18.
  • Bears argue that rising competition, fee pressure, and heavy spending on digital assets could cap revenue growth and make earnings more volatile, and the current data give those concerns some grounding:
    • The combination of a higher P/E than close peers and weaker debt coverage means investors are paying a small premium to the peer group while also accepting a material financing risk, which fits the more cautious take on valuation.
    • Forecast revenue growth of around 7.8% per year and earnings growth of about 11.15% per year are also below broader US market expectations in the dataset. If fee compression or digital spending bites harder than planned, the present earnings base and margin could be harder to maintain than the recent 117.4% earnings growth might suggest.
Given those pressure points, if you want to see how skeptics frame the downside case around fees, competition, and digital spend, 🐻 WisdomTree Bear Case

data.title: The Quiet Compounders Investors Are Not Talking About For Low‑Risk Stability
data.description:
Markets are being pulled in many directions right now, from energy shocks and stubborn inflation to shifting central bank policies and uneven growth signals across the US, Europe and Asia. When so many moving parts affect rates, currencies and input costs, it can help to anchor a portfolio in companies that our model scores as lower risk with resilient balance sheets. This Low-Risk Leaders screener focuses on stocks with the smallest risk scores in the tool, with the goal of reducing portfolio swings without sacrificing potential returns. Below are three names that currently stand out.

Advertisement

Amkor Technology (AMKR)

Overview: Amkor Technology is a global provider of outsourced semiconductor packaging and testing, handling everything from preparing wafers and designing packages to final system testing and shipping for chipmakers and electronics brands across the US, Europe, Japan and Asia Pacific.

Operations: Amkor generates all of its US$7.1b in revenue from semiconductor equipment and services, focused on advanced packaging and testing for a broad range of end markets.

Market Cap: US$17.3b

Amkor can be relevant for investors who want exposure to the semiconductor supply chain with a focus on packaging and test rather than wafer fabrication. Record recent revenues, momentum in advanced packaging for AI hardware and a plan to triple its AI focused portfolio in 2026 sit alongside CHIPS backed US expansion in Arizona and new capacity in Asia. Together, these factors indicate a company aiming to deepen its role with top chip customers. At the same time, reliance on a few large clients, thin margins, aggressive capex and higher funding risk from external borrowing and convertible notes highlight that the growth path involves meaningful trade-offs, which is why a closer look can be valuable.

Record revenues, AI packaging momentum and CHIPS backed expansion are only half the story. Use the analysis report for Amkor Technology to see how funding risk and customer concentration could change the equation.

Intuitive Surgical (ISRG)

Overview: Intuitive Surgical develops robotic systems like the da Vinci Surgical System and Ion endoluminal platform that help doctors perform minimally invasive surgeries and lung biopsies with more precision and smaller incisions in hospitals around the world.

Operations: Intuitive Surgical generates US$10.6b in revenue primarily from surgical and medical equipment, with around US$7.1b from the United States and US$3.5b from international markets.

Market Cap: US$162.1b

Intuitive Surgical earns most of its money not just from selling robots but from the ongoing instruments, services and digital tools that support roughly 9,500 installed da Vinci systems, and this recurring stream is now 83% of revenue. That helps explain the company’s high margins and high quality earnings, even as investors weigh a rich P/E multiple, external funding reliance and regulatory noise such as recent FDA recalls on older instruments. Recent quarters have highlighted double digit procedure growth, strong international uptake and an expanding da Vinci 5 rollout. However, the share price has lagged the broader US market and some analysts argue the stock trades well above estimated fair value. For investors, the key question is whether this recurring revenue engine justifies paying up for quality or calls for patience instead.

Intuitive Surgical’s accelerating procedure growth and 83% recurring revenue can make the rich P/E look less straightforward than it seems. See how the analyst forecasts for Intuitive Surgical stack up against that valuation risk that few are talking about.

Catalyst Pharmaceuticals (CPRX)

Overview: Catalyst Pharmaceuticals is a US based commercial biopharma company that develops and sells treatments for rare neurological and neuromuscular diseases, including LEMS, certain seizure disorders and Duchenne muscular dystrophy through branded drugs such as Firdapse, Fycompa and Agamree.

Operations: Catalyst generates about US$589m in revenue from the development and commercialization of drug products, currently all from the United States.

Market Cap: US$3.4b

Catalyst Pharmaceuticals brings together a concentrated rare disease portfolio, high margins and a solid balance sheet, with growth driven by broader diagnosis, better payer coverage and an accelerating Agamree rollout that helps reduce dependence on Firdapse. At the same time, reliance on a handful of drugs, pending patent litigation around Firdapse, a relatively thin late stage pipeline and rising SG&A tied to an expanded sales force mean the story is not risk free. In addition, the share price sits well below one fair value estimate and there is active M&A interest. Overall, this is a company where the balance between resilience and concentration risk deserves a closer look.

Accelerating rare disease revenues and a solid balance sheet can make Catalyst’s concentration risk easy to underestimate. See how the analysis report for Catalyst Pharmaceuticals ties together patent battles, M&A interest, and one underappreciated twist.

Approach 1: FIGS Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s dollars to estimate what the business might be worth now.

For FIGS, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach that draws on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $46.0 million. Analyst input and extrapolated estimates see free cash flow reaching $56.9 million in 2026 and $74.0 million in 2027, with further projections extending out to 2035, all expressed in $ and adjusted back to today using a discount rate.

Bringing these projected cash flows together, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $7.93 per share for FIGS. Compared with the recent share price of US$15.40, this implies the stock is assessed as about 94.1% above the DCF estimate, suggesting the market price is high relative to this cash flow based calculation.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: FIGS Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to gauge how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. This makes it a straightforward check on whether the share price looks stretched or reasonable.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends a lot on how fast earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are, with higher growth and lower perceived risk usually matching up with higher P/E ratios. FIGS currently trades on a P/E of 75.11x, compared with the Luxury industry average of 19.54x and a peer average of 57.91x, so the stock is priced well above those broad benchmarks.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio framework goes a step further. data.title: Matthews International (MATW) Q2 Loss And Revenue Pressure Challenge Bullish Growth Narrative
data.description:
Matthews International (MATW) has reported Q2 2026 results with revenue of US$258.6 million, a basic EPS loss of US$0.69 and net income loss of US$21.8 million, setting a cautious tone for the quarter. Over the past few quarters, revenue has moved from US$427.6 million in Q2 2025 to US$349.4 million in Q3, US$318.8 million in Q4, US$284.8 million in Q1 2026 and now US$258.6 million in Q2 2026. Basic EPS has swung between losses and profits, including US$0.50 in Q3 2025, a loss of US$0.88 in Q4 2025, US$1.39 in Q1 2026 and back to a loss this quarter. With trailing twelve month EPS of US$0.31 and net income of US$9.7 million, the latest print keeps the spotlight firmly on margins and on how durable any underlying profitability is for shareholders.

See our full analysis for Matthews International.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the most common narratives around Matthews International and where those stories might need updating.

See what the community is saying about Matthews International

Advertisement

TTM profit of US$9.7m leans on US$129m one off

  • Over the last twelve months, Matthews International reported net income of US$9.7 million and basic EPS of US$0.31, but that period includes a US$129.0 million one off gain that materially affects how sustainable that profit looks.
  • What stands out for the bullish narrative is that the move into profitability sits alongside a push into higher margin areas like automation, 2D barcode printheads and battery technologies. However, the reliance on a US$129.0 million non recurring gain means the recent US$9.7 million profit does not on its own prove that these growth themes are already driving clean, repeatable earnings.

Revenue trend contrasts with growth story

  • Quarterly revenue has gone from US$427.6 million in Q2 2025 to US$258.6 million in Q2 2026, and analysts in the provided data also expect revenue to decline further by about 4.4% per year over the next three years. That sits against the idea of a simple top line lift from automation and memorialization growth areas.
  • Bulls highlight demand from warehouse automation, barcode upgrades and battery technology as long term drivers. Yet the recent pattern of revenue moving from US$427.6 million to US$258.6 million and the forecast average 4.4% annual decline show that these growth pockets are currently being offset by pressures in legacy memorialization volumes and other weaker areas, so the bullish case depends on those newer businesses eventually outweighing what is being lost elsewhere.
Curious how that bullish long term story stacks up against the current revenue pressure, and where believers think the real inflection comes from, 🐂 Matthews International Bull Case.

High 91.7x P/E versus mixed cash coverage

  • The stock trades on a trailing P/E of 91.7x, compared with peer and industry averages of 24.8x and 16.5x. At the same time, interest payments and the 3.57% dividend are flagged as not well covered by earnings or free cash flow, which is a tough combination for income focused investors.
  • Bears argue that weak coverage of interest and dividends, together with forecasts for earnings to decline about 9.9% per year and revenue about 4.4% per year, leave little room for error. The very high 91.7x P/E relative to a sector closer to 16.5x strengthens the cautious view that current pricing and payouts lean heavily on optimistic assumptions despite the company only posting US$9.7 million of trailing net income.
If you are wondering how skeptics justify that caution given the headline profitability and high multiple, the fuller bearish case lays out the detailed concerns in one place 🐻 Matthews International Bear Case.

data.title: Is MetLife (MET) Now At An Interesting Price After Strong Recent Share Gains?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether MetLife at around US$80.23 is offering fair value or reflects a potential mismatch between price and business quality, this breakdown will help you frame that question clearly.
  • The stock has returned 3.3% over the last 7 days and 12.7% over the last 30 days, with a 1 year return of 6.2% and a 3 year return of 61.1%.
  • Recent news around MetLife has focused on its positioning within the wider US insurance sector and how its scale and product mix compare to peers. These updates give context for why investors may be reassessing both risk and growth potential even though the year to date return is around a 0.1% decline.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation framework, MetLife currently has a value score of 2. The sections that follow walk through the main valuation methods behind that score and suggest a broader way to think about value that ties everything together by the end of the article.

MetLife scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: MetLife Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model evaluates how much profit a company is expected to generate above the return that equity investors require, and then capitalizes those extra profits into an intrinsic value per share.

For MetLife, the starting point is an estimated Book Value of $43.33 per share and a Stable EPS of $8.62 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 8 analysts. The average Return on Equity is 15.85%, compared with a Cost of Equity of $4.33 per share, which implies an Excess Return of $4.29 per share. In simple terms, the model assumes MetLife can earn more on its equity base than investors require.

The analysis also uses a Stable Book Value estimate of $54.39 per share, sourced from weighted future Book Value estimates from 6 analysts, to project how those excess returns build over time. Combining these inputs, the Excess Returns valuation produces an intrinsic value of about $148.44 per share, which indicates the stock is 46.0% undervalued relative to a share price around $80.23.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests MetLife is undervalued by 46.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 51 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for MetLife.

Approach 2: MetLife Price vs Earnings

P/E is a common way to value profitable companies because it links what you pay for each share to the earnings that support that share. It gives you a quick sense of how many dollars of price you are paying for each dollar of current earnings.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E ratio usually reflects how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risks. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while lower growth or higher risk tends to go with a lower P/E.

MetLife currently trades on a P/E of 16.31x. This compares with an Insurance industry average P/E of 11.72x and a peer group average of 13.21x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for MetLife, which is 14.55x, is a proprietary estimate of what P/E might make sense given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

Because the Fair Ratio incorporates these fundamentals directly, it can be more informative than a simple comparison with peers or the broader industry.

With the actual P/E of 16.31x sitting above the Fair Ratio of 14.55x by more than 0.10, the stock screens as overvalued on this metric alone.

Result: OVERVALUED
